stories.headline: Denver Sheriff recruit dies after falling ill during academy physical training
stories.summary:
A Denver Sheriff Department recruit has died after becoming ill during physical training at the department's training academy. Dorbor Mulbah expressed discomfort and became unwell following the training exercise on Tuesday and was transported to a local hospital, where he died Wednesday morning.

The Denver Sheriff Department confirmed the death and said Mulbah received medical care before he passed. No additional details about the specific cause of death or the nature of the training exercise were immediately released.

The incident is a significant development for the department, which has faced ongoing scrutiny over its training and staffing practices. Authorities have not indicated whether a formal investigation into the circumstances of his death has been launched.

stories.headline: Colorado faces 'very challenging' wildfire season as drought covers entire state, officials warn
stories.summary:
State fire and emergency management leaders warned Thursday that Colorado is facing one of its most dangerous wildfire seasons in recent memory, driven by a dry winter, record-low snowpack and an abnormally warm spring. The Front Range and northwestern Colorado face the highest risk through June and July, Gov. Jared Polis said at a news conference held at Rocky Mountain Metropolitan Airport near Broomfield.

As of Tuesday, drought covered 100% of Colorado, according to the U.S. Drought Monitor. Much of the state's northwest corner is in exceptional drought — the most severe category — while the Denver metro is classified as being in extreme drought, the second-most-severe level. At the same point last year, only 44% of the state was in any drought condition.

Mike Morgan, director of the Colorado Division of Fire Prevention and Control, said the state will not allow any fires to burn longer than necessary this year, a departure from past practice of allowing some fires to continue if they posed no threat. He also warned that firefighting resources will be stretched thin across the West, making shared federal assets harder to access.

Climate researcher Dominik Kulakowski of Clark University noted that drier conditions make ignition easier and allow fires to spread more rapidly through forests. Colorado has significantly expanded its own firefighting capacity in recent years, including purchasing aircraft, but officials said that investment will be tested by the severity of this year's outlook.

stories.headline: Police opposition kills two Colorado data privacy bills targeting Flock cameras and warrantless data access
stories.summary:
Two bipartisan bills aimed at protecting Coloradans from warrantless government data collection were defeated in the past week after running into stiff resistance from law enforcement groups and, in one case, a veto threat from Gov. Jared Polis. Senate Bill 70, which would have limited government access to data from license plate-reading camera networks such as those operated by Flock Safety, was voluntarily withdrawn by its sponsors on the Senate floor Wednesday.

House Bill 1037, which would have asked voters to weigh in on whether law enforcement could access individuals' data from third-party brokers without a warrant, was killed the previous week in the House Judiciary Committee. Both bills had brought together unusual coalitions, pairing progressive civil rights groups with conservative organizations including Rocky Mountain Gun Owners.

Sen. Judy Amabile, a Boulder Democrat sponsoring SB-70, lamented from the Senate floor that constituents are demanding action on data privacy concerns, saying residents should not have their movements constantly tracked simply for going about daily life. Rep. Jennifer Bacon, a Denver Democrat who co-sponsored HB-1037, said the legislature proved unable or unwilling to seriously weigh public concerns about government surveillance.

Police officers, chiefs, sheriffs and district attorneys all opposed the measures, arguing they would hamper criminal investigations. The defeats come as polls show broad public concern about mass data collection — a 2023 Pew Research survey found 71% of Americans worried about government use of citizens' data.

stories.headline: Aurora Police Department unlikely to reach full consent decree compliance by February 2027 deadline, monitor says
stories.summary:
The independent monitor overseeing Aurora's police consent decree has concluded that the Aurora Police Department will likely not achieve full compliance with all mandated reforms before the decree's scheduled end date of Feb. 15, 2027. The finding came in the tenth of twelve expected monitoring reports, covering the period from August 2025 through February 2026.

The consent decree, initiated after the 2019 death of Elijah McClain during a police encounter, outlines 78 mandates covering use of force, racial bias, officer accountability and transparency. As of the latest reporting period, 63 of those mandates were in substantial compliance. Outstanding areas include accountability policies, bias training and transparency requirements.

Despite the shortfall, the monitor noted that APD's trajectory remains positive. A stakeholder survey of approximately 1,000 people showed fewer negative perceptions of the department compared to the same survey conducted in 2022. Aurora Fire Rescue was separately found to be in substantial compliance with requirements related to the use of ketamine and other chemical sedatives, concluding that portion of the oversight.

The monitor, IntegrAssure, said it will work with the city and the Colorado Attorney General's Office during the next reporting period to determine how to address the anticipated non-compliance scenario before the decree expires.

stories.headline: Update: Nuggets eliminated from playoffs as Timberwolves win Game 6
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets were eliminated from the NBA playoffs after falling to the Minnesota Timberwolves in Game 6, ending their postseason run in the first round. The loss caps a season marked by both high expectations and inconsistency for a team that reached the NBA Finals two years ago.

Denver had rallied to force a Game 6 after winning Game 5 to pull within 3-2 in the series, but could not extend the series to a seventh game. Fans and observers reflected on a turbulent season that saw the Nuggets navigate injuries and roster questions before ultimately falling short in the first round.

The elimination raises questions about the team's roster construction and direction heading into the offseason, including the futures of key contributors and the coaching staff's approach. No further details on specific game statistics or a final score for Game 6 were immediately available in published reports.

stories.headline: RTD used Denver disability advocate's likeness on bus wrap without her consent
stories.summary:
Denver musician, activist and former mayoral candidate Kalyn Heffernan said she was shocked to discover her image displayed on Regional Transportation District buses without her knowledge or permission. She learned of the bus wrap when someone sent her a photo of the vehicle, which featured an artist's rendering based on an older photograph of her.

The artwork was created by artist Justin Bravo and selected through an RTD community contest to commemorate the anniversary of the Americans with Disabilities Act of 1990. A panel of community members representing disability and accessibility organizations chose the design from six submissions. RTD said it was unaware the artwork depicted Heffernan's likeness until recently, and Bravo issued an apology after learning of the issue, saying his goal was to honor history and amplify underserved voices.

Heffernan, who is a visibly disabled person and a regular public transit rider, called the situation exploitative and particularly frustrating given RTD's recent announcement that it is considering cutting bus and rail service by 20% to address a $200 million budget deficit. 'I rely on it the most and I'm being exploited by it,' she said.

RTD said the bus wrap is scheduled to be removed at the end of July and that there are no plans to take it down earlier. Heffernan has not indicated whether she intends to pursue any legal action.

stories.headline: Film on the Rocks announces 2026 season at Red Rocks, featuring 'Sinners,' 'The Princess Bride' and 'Jurassic Park'
stories.summary:
Denver Film has announced the 27th season of Film on the Rocks at Red Rocks Amphitheatre, with five screenings scheduled from June through August 2026. The season opens Monday, June 8, with Rob Reiner's 1987 classic 'The Princess Bride' and closes Aug. 10 with 'Jurassic Park.'

The lineup also includes Ryan Coogler's 2026 release 'Sinners' and a 20th anniversary screening of 'Little Miss Sunshine,' the latter featuring a live performance by Denver band DeVotchKa, which contributed to the film's iconic soundtrack. The 'Little Miss Sunshine' screening represents a collaboration between Denver Film and the Sundance Film Festival, which is set to make its Boulder debut in January.

Tickets are priced at $25 general admission and $50 for VIP reserved seating near the front of the amphitheater — the same prices as last year. Public tickets go on sale Friday, May 1, at 10 a.m. through denverfilm.org, with Denver Film members having access now.

Gates open at 6:30 p.m. for each screening, with pre-show entertainment beginning at 7 p.m. and films starting at 8:30 p.m. Local comedian Janae Burris will again serve as emcee for all five events.

stories.headline: Mountain snow and Denver thunderstorms expected Thursday as spring storm moves through Colorado
stories.summary:
A spring storm system brought rain to Denver on Thursday morning and was forecast to produce afternoon thunderstorms in the metro area between 1 p.m. and 5 p.m., according to the National Weather Service. Rain was expected to remain possible through 5 p.m. Friday, though no snow accumulation was forecast for the Denver metro.

In the mountains, widespread snow was expected to continue through Friday morning, with the heaviest totals anticipated in the Front Range mountains south of Interstate 70. A winter weather advisory was in effect from 6 a.m. Thursday through 6 a.m. Friday for portions of Boulder, Chaffee, Clear Creek, Fremont, Gilpin, Grand, Jackson, Lake, Larimer, Park and Summit counties.

Mountain travel was expected to be hazardous during the advisory period. The storm arrives as Colorado fire officials separately warned that the state's overall precipitation deficit remains severe and will have little impact on the elevated wildfire risk expected this summer.

stories.headline: Red Robin hires new executive as Greenwood Village-based chain pursues turnaround
stories.summary:
Red Robin Gourmet Burgers has added a restaurant industry veteran to its C-suite as the Greenwood Village-based burger chain continues efforts to reverse a period of declining performance. Details of the specific role and the executive's identity were not fully disclosed in available reporting.

Red Robin has been working to stabilize its business amid broader challenges facing casual dining chains, including changing consumer habits and increased competition. The hire signals continued management-level investment in the company's recovery strategy.

The company is headquartered in the Denver metro area and operates hundreds of locations nationally, making its financial trajectory a matter of local business interest.

stories.headline: Denver's climate office proposes changes to Energize Denver building performance rules for restaurants and condos
stories.summary:
Denver's climate office has proposed modifications to the Energize Denver program's building performance standards, targeting implementation challenges faced by restaurants and condominium properties. The proposal aims to address difficulties some building types have encountered in meeting the program's energy efficiency benchmarks.

Energize Denver requires commercial and multifamily buildings above a certain size to meet escalating energy performance targets over time, with the goal of reducing the city's greenhouse gas emissions. Restaurant operations and condo ownership structures have posed particular compliance complications since the program took effect.

The proposed rule changes were flagged in reporting by the Denver Business Journal as city officials work to make the program more workable without undermining its environmental objectives. Further details on the specific modifications proposed were not immediately available.

stories.headline: Cinco de Mayo Festival, Kentucky Derby party, Supercross and more highlight Denver weekend events May 1-3
stories.summary:
Denver's weekend calendar is packed with major events from May 1 through May 3, headlined by an early Cinco de Mayo Festival at Civic Center Park on Saturday and Sunday from 10 a.m. to 8 p.m. The free festival will feature food, drinks, Chihuahua races, a taco eating contest, a low-rider car show and activities for children.

McGregor Square will host the 25th and final Denver Derby Party on Saturday starting at 1 p.m., marking the end of a 25-year tradition with the Kentucky Derby broadcast on the venue's large outdoor LED screen. The Monster Energy AMA Supercross Championship also comes to Empower Field at Mile High on Saturday, with racing beginning at 4:30 p.m.

Denver Fashion Week opens Friday at the Furniture Row Showroom on Bannock Street and runs through May 9, featuring eight runway shows including a new outerwear and athleisure category. First Friday Art Walks return to Denver's five arts districts on Friday evening, and First Friday Jazz will fill the Welton Street Corridor in Five Points from 5 p.m. to 2 a.m.

stories.headline: Broncos land in top tier of NFL power rankings following draft
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos have been ranked among the top teams in NFL power rankings following their activity in the 2026 NFL Draft, according to reporting aggregated by Mile High Report. The strong ranking reflects positive assessments of Denver's draft class and overall roster moves this offseason.

The Broncos were previously reported to have been active in the offseason with draft night results drawing comparisons across the AFC West. The power ranking placement suggests analysts view Denver as a legitimate contender heading into the 2026 season.

Specific details on the ranking source and Denver's precise position were not fully detailed in available reporting, but the development adds to a series of encouraging offseason indicators for the franchise.

weather.notable: Nearly 9 degrees below the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: There is no such thing as fun for the whole family.

weekendEvents.title: Five Points Live – First Friday Jazz
weekendEvents.description: The Five Points cultural district celebrates First Friday with live jazz across multiple venues along the Welton Street Corridor, featuring performers including the Elisa Garcia Quartet, Tenia Nelson Trio, Gregory Goodloe, and more.
weekendEvents.location: Welton Street Corridor, Five Points, Denver

weekendEvents.title: Denver Cinco de Mayo Festival
weekendEvents.description: One of the largest Cinco de Mayo festivals in the country, this annual event at Civic Center Park draws more than 300,000 attendees with food, music, and cultural celebrations highlighting Denver's Latino community.

weekendEvents.title: Small Wonders Weekend – Colorado Railroad Museum
weekendEvents.description: The Colorado Railroad Museum hosts a model train event featuring a modular O gauge layout by the High Plains 3-Railers, plus steam train rides offered daily from 10 a.m. to 3 p.m.
weekendEvents.location: Colorado Railroad Museum, 17155 West 44th Avenue, Golden

weekendEvents.title: Pueblo Vida: Cinco De Mayo Culture Fest
weekendEvents.description: A four-day multicultural celebration and soft opening of a new cultural space at the Joe Molina Gallery, featuring an art market, live music, poetry, singing, dancing, and food vendors.
weekendEvents.location: Joe Molina Gallery, 2217 Welton Street, Denver

weekendEvents.title: La Raza Park Grand Reopening
weekendEvents.description: The City of Denver celebrates the completion of major renovations to La Raza Park with a ribbon cutting, a traditional blessing, a performance by Grupo Tlaloc Danza Azteca, and music by the Bryant Webster Elementary School Mariachi Band.
weekendEvents.location: La Raza Park, 1501 West 38th Avenue, Denver

weekendEvents.title: Destination Colfax Premiere
weekendEvents.description: A film premiere celebrating the history of Colfax Avenue, held at the Fox Arts Center on East Colfax.
weekendEvents.location: Fox Arts Center, 9900 East Colfax Avenue, Denver

weekendEvents.title: Black Veil Brides with From Ashes To New, TX2, and As December Falls
weekendEvents.description: Black Veil Brides headline a rock show at the Fillmore Auditorium, joined by From Ashes To New, TX2, and As December Falls.

weekendEvents.title: Madama Butterfly – Opera Colorado
weekendEvents.description: Opera Colorado presents Puccini's Madama Butterfly, the emotionally charged story of Cio-Cio San and her devotion to an American naval officer, in its return to the Denver stage.
weekendEvents.location: Denver Center for the Performing Arts, Denver

weekendEvents.title: Stories on Stage: Greatest Hits
weekendEvents.description: Stories on Stage closes out its 25th season with favorite stories from past years, performed by Randy Moore, Kate Gleason, and Gareth Saxe at Su Teatro.
weekendEvents.location: Su Teatro Cultural and Performing Arts Center, 721 Santa Fe Drive, Denver

weekendEvents.title: Mile High Asian Food Week
weekendEvents.description: A week-long culinary celebration supporting Colorado's Asian American, Native Hawaiian, and Pacific Islander community, with participating restaurants offering special deals and secret menu items through Sunday.

weekendEvents.title: Cinco de Mayo en Westwood
weekendEvents.description: A neighborhood Cinco de Mayo celebration in Denver's Westwood community over the weekend.

weekendEvents.title: Estes Park Duck Race Festival
weekendEvents.description: An annual community festival in Estes Park featuring a rubber duck race and accompanying festivities.

topStory.headline: Iran's Supreme Leader Vows to Defend Nuclear and Missile Programs as U.S. Blockade Squeezes Economy
topStory.summary:
Iran's Supreme Leader Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei on Thursday vowed to protect the Islamic Republic's nuclear and missile capabilities, issuing a defiant statement as the United States presses for a broader agreement to solidify a fragile ceasefire. In remarks read by a state television anchor, Khamenei declared that the only place Americans belonged in the Persian Gulf was "at the bottom of its waters" and said a "new chapter" was being written in the region's history. Khamenei has not appeared in public since assuming the role of supreme leader following the death of his father, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, in the opening airstrikes of the conflict.

The statement comes as Iran's economy faces severe strain on multiple fronts. A U.S. Navy blockade has halted Iranian oil tankers from reaching international markets, and Iran continues to restrict passage through the Strait of Hormuz, through which approximately one-fifth of the world's crude oil flows. The global benchmark, Brent crude, reached as high as $126 a barrel on Thursday, and the average price of gasoline in the United States climbed to $4.39 per gallon on Friday, up 34 cents from a week earlier, according to AAA.

Iran's approximately 90 million residents have been cut off from the global internet for most of 2026 in what authorities have described as a wartime measure, one of the longest and strictest such national shutdowns on record. The blackout is estimated to cost the Iranian economy $30 million to $40 million daily in direct losses, with indirect losses likely double that figure, according to a member of Iran's Chamber of Commerce. About 10 million Iranians hold jobs dependent on internet connectivity, the country's communications minister has said.

The United States is pursuing a diplomatic initiative it calls a "maritime freedom construct" aimed at reopening the Strait of Hormuz to international shipping. A State Department cable sent Tuesday instructed American diplomats in most countries — excluding those in Belarus, China, Cuba and Russia — to seek host government support for the plan, which would maintain the blockade on Iranian ports while coordinating allied pressure against Iranian efforts to disrupt energy flows. The initiative is being led jointly by the State Department and U.S. Central Command.

President Trump, in an interview with Newsmax, again claimed the United States had won the war but said he wanted to "win by a bigger margin," asserting that Iran's navy and air force had been destroyed. The administration is also conducting a review of U.S. troop levels in Europe, with questions raised about the future of forces stationed in Germany, Italy and Spain, countries that have at times clashed with Washington over the use of their bases for Iran-related military operations.
